content.text: Consuming a REST API means writing client code that sends HTTP requests to a remote server, receives structured responses, and integrates that data into the calling application. REST — Representational State Transfer — is an architectural style, not a protocol. It uses standard HTTP methods as verbs (
), URL paths as resource addresses, and HTTP status codes to communicate outcome. Responses are almost always JSON in modern APIs, occasionally XML or plain text.

In the context of agentic AI systems, REST APIs are how agents talk to the outside world. An agent might call a weather API to get the current temperature, a search API to retrieve web results, or a database API to look up customer records. Every tool the agent uses is typically a thin wrapper around one or more REST API calls. Understanding how to write correct, robust HTTP client code is therefore foundational to building reliable agents.

The client side of a REST interaction involves four concerns: constructing a well-formed request (URL, method, headers, body); authenticating the request so the server trusts the caller; handling the response (parsing JSON, checking the status code, surfacing errors); and dealing with the real-world constraints of HTTP at scale — rate limits, retries, pagination, and caching.

Nearly every capability an agentic AI system needs beyond pure language generation comes from an external API: web search, code execution, database lookup, calendar management, payment processing, file storage. The LLM reasons about what to do; the HTTP client actually does it. A poorly written HTTP client — one that does not handle errors, retries blindly, leaks credentials, or silently drops paginated results — will cause the agent to fail in production even if its prompting and reasoning logic are perfect.

Correctness in HTTP client code has a direct user-visible impact. A tool function that crashes on an HTTP 429 rate-limit response will abort the agent's current task. One that truncates paginated results will give the agent incomplete information and produce wrong answers. One that embeds an API key in source code will eventually expose it in a public repository. These are not theoretical risks — they are among the most common real-world failures in agentic systems.

REST APIs expose capabilities from virtually every major platform. An agent that can consume REST can interact with GitHub, Slack, Google Maps, Stripe, and thousands of other services using the same underlying skill.
Each REST request is self-contained. The client does not need to maintain a persistent connection or track server-side session state. This maps cleanly onto the stateless nature of LLM tool calls.
handle the low-level HTTP mechanics, leaving the application code to focus on business logic.
URL paths encode resource identity in a way that is easy to read, log, and debug.
REST calls can be mocked at the HTTP transport layer, enabling fast deterministic unit tests of tool functions without network access.

REST does not define a strict schema for request and response bodies. Each API chooses its own JSON shape, pagination style, authentication mechanism, and error format. Calling three different APIs means learning three different conventions. GraphQL and gRPC address this by enforcing a typed schema, at the cost of higher implementation complexity.
REST servers can return any JSON shape. A client must validate responses defensively or risk runtime errors when the API changes.
Public APIs impose request quotas. An agent loop that issues hundreds of tool calls per minute will be throttled. Every client must implement back-off logic.
Large collections are spread across multiple pages. Fetching all results requires a loop that follows cursor or
headers — code that every client must write.
Each HTTP round trip adds 20–500 ms of network latency. An agent that chains ten tool calls in sequence can accumulate multiple seconds of overhead before returning to the user.
Credential management, token refresh (for OAuth), and secret rotation are operational concerns that the client code must handle correctly.
An HTTP client sends a request message to a server over TCP (or TLS for HTTPS). The request has four parts: a method line (
), an optional body (for POST/PUT/PATCH), and a blank line separating headers from body. The server responds with a status line (
), response headers, and the response body.

HTTP status codes follow a three-digit pattern. Codes in the 2xx range indicate success: 200 OK (request succeeded), 201 Created (resource was created), 204 No Content (success with no body). Codes in the 4xx range are client errors: 400 Bad Request (malformed input), 401 Unauthorized (missing or invalid credentials), 403 Forbidden (authenticated but not permitted), 404 Not Found (resource does not exist), 429 Too Many Requests (rate limit hit). Codes in the 5xx range are server errors: 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable. Client code must handle each class differently.
raises an exception for any 4xx or 5xx code, making error detection trivial.
The diagram shows how a request flows from the agent or client through the HTTP Methods layer to the REST API server, and back. The
injects credentials into every outbound request. The
response headers and applies exponential back-off when HTTP 429 is returned. The
deserializes JSON and validates it against a Pydantic model. The
maps 4xx/5xx status codes to typed exceptions. HTTP Cache (dashed, optional) uses

REST API consumption is appropriate whenever the agent needs to interact with a service that exposes an HTTP interface. This covers the vast majority of external integrations. Use a direct HTTP client when there is no purpose-built Python SDK for the service, or when the SDK is too heavyweight to justify as a dependency.
calling any external web service: search engines, mapping services, weather APIs, social media platforms, payment processors, notification services.
the service provides an OpenAPI/Swagger specification — you can generate a typed Python client automatically and call it from your tool functions.
when communicating with an internal microservice where binary efficiency and strict typing matter more than human readability.
when the agent needs to fetch deeply nested, flexible data structures and the server exposes a GraphQL endpoint.
(e.g., the Anthropic Python SDK, Stripe's Python client) when one is available and well-maintained — it handles auth, retries, and pagination for you.
Read the API documentation. Identify the base URL, authentication method, available endpoints, request parameters, response schema, and rate-limit policy before writing any code.
Store credentials in environment variables or a secrets manager. Never hardcode API keys or tokens in source files or notebooks.
) with the base URL and default headers set once, then reuse it across all calls to the same API. This enables connection pooling and eliminates repeated header setup.
to handle 429 (retry after back-off) and 4xx (surface to agent as an error) separately from 5xx (retry with back-off).
Parse the JSON response into a Pydantic model. This validates the shape and gives you typed attribute access instead of fragile dictionary key lookups.
If the endpoint is paginated, write a generator that follows the
header automatically and yields individual items to the caller.
Wrap the entire HTTP client in a class and inject it into your agent as a dependency. This makes it mockable in tests.
Basic GET request with error handling

# pip install httpx
import os
import httpx

def get_user(user_id: int) -> dict:
    """Fetch a single user from the JSONPlaceholder demo API."""
    api_key = os.environ["DEMO_API_KEY"]   # loaded from environment, never hardcoded

    with httpx.Client(
        base_url="https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"},
        timeout=10.0,
    ) as client:
        response = client.get(f"/users/{user_id}")
        response.raise_for_status()        # raises HTTPStatusError for 4xx/5xx
        return response.json()


# ---- calling it ----
try:
    user = get_user(1)
    print(user["name"])              # "Leanne Graham"
except httpx.HTTPStatusError as e:
    print(f"API error {e.response.status_code}: {e.response.text}")
except httpx.RequestError as e:
    # network-level failure (DNS, timeout, connection refused)
    print(f"Network error: {e}")
Authenticated client with retry and back-off

import os, time, httpx
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Generator

# --- Pydantic model for type-safe response parsing ---
class Repo(BaseModel):
    id: int
    name: str
    full_name: str
    stargazers_count: int
    html_url: str


class GitHubClient:
    """Reusable client for the GitHub REST API (v3)."""

    BASE_URL = "https://api.github.com"
    MAX_RETRIES = 4

    def __init__(self, token: str) -> None:
        self._client = httpx.Client(
            base_url=self.BASE_URL,
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
                "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
                "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
            },
            timeout=15.0,
        )

    def _request(self, method: str, path: str, **kwargs) -> httpx.Response:
        """Issue a request with exponential back-off on 429 and 5xx."""
        delay = 1.0
        for attempt in range(self.MAX_RETRIES):
            resp = self._client.request(method, path, **kwargs)
            if resp.status_code == 429:
                retry_after = float(resp.headers.get("Retry-After", delay))
                time.sleep(retry_after)
                delay *= 2
                continue
            if resp.status_code >= 500 and attempt < self.MAX_RETRIES - 1:
                time.sleep(delay)
                delay *= 2
                continue
            resp.raise_for_status()
            return resp
        resp.raise_for_status()   # let last attempt bubble up
        return resp               # unreachable, but satisfies type checkers

    def search_repos(self, query: str, per_page: int = 10) -> list[Repo]:
        """Search GitHub repositories and return Pydantic-validated results."""
        resp = self._request(
            "GET", "/search/repositories",
            params={"q": query, "per_page": per_page, "sort": "stars"},
        )
        items = resp.json()["items"]
        return [Repo.model_validate(item) for item in items]

    def close(self) -> None:
        self._client.close()


# ---- usage ----
client = GitHubClient(token=os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"])
repos = client.search_repos("agentic AI", per_page=5)
for repo in repos:
    print(f"{repo.full_name:50s} {repo.stargazers_count:>6} stars")
client.close()

import httpx
from typing import Generator

def iter_github_issues(
    owner: str, repo: str, token: str, state: str = "open"
) -> Generator[dict, None, None]:
    """
    Yield every issue from a GitHub repository, following pagination automatically.

    GitHub uses a 'Link' header with rel="next" to point to the next page.
    Stopping when that header is absent collects the complete result set.
    """
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues"
    params = {"state": state, "per_page": 100}
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
    }

    with httpx.Client(timeout=15.0) as client:
        while url:
            resp = client.get(url, params=params, headers=headers)
            resp.raise_for_status()
            yield from resp.json()           # yield each issue dict individually

            # GitHub pagination: Link: <https://...?page=2>; rel="next"
            link_header = resp.headers.get("link", "")
            next_url = None
            for part in link_header.split(","):
                if 'rel="next"' in part:
                    next_url = part.split(";")[0].strip().strip("<>")
                    break
            url = next_url    # None stops the loop
            params = {}       # next URL already has all params baked in


# ---- count all open issues without loading them all into memory ----
count = sum(1 for _ in iter_github_issues("anthropics", "anthropic-sdk-python", token="..."))
print(f"{count} open issues")

Consider an agent that answers questions about a repository by querying the GitHub API. The agent receives a user question like "How many open issues does the anthropic-sdk-python repo have tagged 'bug'?". Its tool function calls the GitHub search API, handles pagination to count all matching issues, and returns the number. Here is how that tool is wired into a real workflow:
The GitHub search API returns a maximum of 30 items per page and includes the total count in a
field on the first response. Rather than paginating through all results, the tool reads

directly — a pattern that avoids unnecessary network calls for this specific query type. For queries that require iterating the full result set (such as computing an aggregate over issue bodies), the paginating generator approach from the examples section is more appropriate.
In production, this tool function is registered with the LLM as a tool definition. When the user asks the question, the LLM emits a tool-use block calling

. The orchestration loop calls the Python function, returns the integer result as a tool result message, and the LLM produces the final answer. The entire exchange takes one HTTP request and one LLM call.
The most widespread mistake is calling
. A 404 response may still return a JSON error body (
succeeds while silently returning an error payload that the application code mistakes for a successful result.
Embedding API keys directly in source code (
) is a critical security error. Keys in source files end up in version control history, notebooks shared with colleagues, and CI logs. Always load secrets from
or a secrets manager at runtime.
Creating a new HTTP client on every call
inside a tool function that is called hundreds of times per agent session opens and closes a TCP connection on every call. Use a long-lived
instance (as a class attribute or a module-level singleton) to enable connection reuse, which reduces latency by 50–200 ms per call.

If a list endpoint returns 30 items per page and the tool function returns only the first page, the agent operates on a truncated dataset and may give incorrect answers. Always check whether a response includes a next-page cursor or
header, and either paginate automatically or document the limitation explicitly in the tool description so the LLM can reason about it.

A 400 Bad Request or 401 Unauthorized will not resolve by retrying — the request itself is the problem. Retry logic should apply only to 429 Too Many Requests (transient quota exhaustion) and 5xx Server Error (transient server failure). Retrying on 4xx errors wastes time and quota.
per external API at startup and reuse it. Set the base URL and default headers once on the client rather than repeating them on every call.
prevents the agent from hanging indefinitely on a slow or unresponsive API.
Parsing the raw dict into a model immediately surfaces schema drift. When the API changes a field name, you get a clear
Jitter (randomising the sleep duration within a range) prevents a thundering herd when multiple agent instances hit a rate limit simultaneously.
When an API returns HTTP 429 with a
header, honour it exactly rather than using your own fixed delay.
Log requests and responses at DEBUG level.
Include URL, method, status code, and response time. This makes it easy to diagnose failures in production without re-running the agent.
Write tool descriptions that include limitations.
If a tool returns at most 10 results, say so in the tool's description. The LLM will factor this into its reasoning and avoid asking for data the tool cannot provide.

The dominant cost of REST calls in agentic systems is network latency, not CPU. A single HTTPS round trip to an API server in a different region can take 100–400 ms. An agent that issues tool calls sequentially pays this cost on every call. Two strategies reduce the impact:
When the agent's reasoning step results in multiple independent tool calls, issue them concurrently using
or a thread pool. Anthropic's API supports returning multiple tool-use blocks in a single response, enabling the orchestrator to dispatch them in parallel.
maintains a pool of keep-alive connections. The first request pays the TLS handshake overhead (~100 ms); subsequent requests on the same connection skip it, saving significant latency at scale.
If the same resource is fetched multiple times within an agent session (e.g., repository metadata used in several steps), cache it in memory using a dict keyed by URL. Honour the API's
enables non-blocking HTTP calls, which is essential for agents that manage dozens of concurrent tool calls. The API is identical to
The modern Python HTTP client. Supports both sync and async, HTTP/2, connection pooling, and streaming responses. Preferred over
The classic sync-only HTTP library. Extremely battle-tested, available everywhere, but lacks async support. Still appropriate for simple scripts and small agents.
Validates JSON responses against typed Python models. Version 2 is significantly faster than v1 and is the standard for data validation in modern Python projects.
A Python library for retry logic. Provides
decorators with configurable stop conditions, wait strategies (exponential, fixed, random), and retry predicates. Cleaner than hand-rolling retry loops.
Generates a typed Python client from an OpenAPI 3 specification. When an API provides a spec, this eliminates the need to write request-building and response-parsing code by hand.
calls at the transport layer and returns pre-configured responses, enabling fast unit tests of tool functions without real network access.
Interactive API exploration tools. Use them to understand an API's response shape and test authentication before writing client code.

API keys are credentials equivalent in power to a password. They must be treated with the same discipline: stored in environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Actions secrets), rotated regularly, scoped to the minimum permissions needed, and never committed to version control. A leaked API key can result in unauthorized API usage charged to your account, exposure of customer data, or supply-chain attacks if the key has write access to repositories or cloud resources.
Never send credentials or sensitive data over plain HTTP. Most APIs reject plain HTTP outright, but a misconfigured client URL (
) is a subtle and dangerous bug.
to disable certificate verification. This opens the connection to man-in-the-middle attacks. If you encounter certificate errors in development, fix the certificate, not the verification flag.
If a tool only reads data, use a read-only API key. A key used by an agent for read-only search should not also have write permissions that could accidentally modify or delete data.
Logging request/response details is essential for debugging but must not log the
header value. Strip or mask credential headers in log output.

OAuth access tokens expire. A robust client detects a 401 response, refreshes the access token using the refresh token, and retries the original request automatically — without surfacing the token refresh to the calling agent.

If an API returns 5xx errors for many consecutive requests, stop retrying and fail fast. Continuing to hammer a failing API wastes quota, slows the agent, and may worsen the server's condition. Libraries like
Think of a REST API as a
. Each resource type (users, orders, products) has its own clearly labelled aisle (URL path). The HTTP method is the instruction you give at the front desk:
means "show me item 42 from the users aisle";
means "add this new item to the orders aisle";
means "remove item 17 from the carts aisle". The response status code is the receipt you get back: 200 (here it is), 404 (no such item), 429 (the warehouse is overwhelmed, come back in 30 seconds).

Your badge — the API key or token — is checked at the entrance. Without it, the desk clerk turns you away with a 401. With the wrong badge for the aisle you are trying to access, you get a 403. And if you try to add an item using the wrong form (malformed JSON body), you get a 400 before anyone even looks at the aisle. The warehouse does not remember your previous visits — each request is self-contained — which is what "stateless" means in REST.

Your HTTP client code is the courier. It carries your request to the warehouse, handles the receipt, and brings the goods back. A good courier knows what to do when the warehouse is temporarily overwhelmed (wait and retry), when the goods are spread across multiple delivery trucks (pagination), and never carries your badge in a visible pocket (keeps credentials out of logs and source code).
REST uses HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) as operations on URL-addressed resources; the response status code communicates success or failure class.
before parsing the response body — a 4xx/5xx response may still return valid JSON that does not represent a successful result.
Load credentials from environment variables at runtime. Never embed API keys or tokens in source code, notebooks, or configuration files committed to version control.
instance per API to benefit from connection pooling and avoid reconnection overhead on every call.
Implement exponential back-off with jitter for 429 and 5xx responses; retry on 4xx errors is almost always wrong.
Paginate completely — do not return only the first page of results from a tool function, as this gives the LLM a misleadingly partial view of the data.
Parse JSON responses into Pydantic models immediately; this catches API schema drift early and gives the rest of the codebase typed, validated data.
For agentic workloads with many concurrent tool calls, switch from
